tate of CaliforJlia 
l~ir Political Practices CommissioIL 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Bert Glennon, Jr. 
City Attorney 
9770 Culver Boulevard 
Culver City, CA 
90230 
Administration 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
January 6, 1984 
Enforcement 
322-60441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-271 
Dear Mr. Glennon: 
ThanK you for your request for advice on Government Code 
Section 84308(a) .ll This section provides that: 
No ••• appOinted officer ••• who.serves on a 
quasi-judicial ••• commission, other than those 
governmental bodies specified in subdivision (d) ,2/ 
shall accept, solicit, or direc~ a contribution of 
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more, from any 
person, or his or her agent, who has an application for 
a license, permit, or other entitlement for use pending 
before any body of which the officer • • • is a member 
and for three months following the date a decision is 
rendered on the application or from any person, or his 
or her agent, who actively supports or opposes the 
application; provided, however, that the officer • 
knows or has reason to Know that the person who 
supports or opposes the application has a financial 
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
2/ Section 84308(d) states: 
Legislative bodies such as city councils, county 
boards of supervisors, and the State Legislature while 
acting as a body or a committee thereof are exempted 
from the coverage of this section •••• 
1/ These terms are explained in the enclosed copy of 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.6. 


Bert Glennon, Jr. 
January 6, 1984 
Page 2 
interest •••• 
This prohibition shall apply regardless 
of whether the officer ••• accepts, solicits, or 
directs the contribution for himself ••• or on behalf 
of any otner officer, alternate, or candidate for 
office. 
In your letter, you asked whether Section 84308(a) allows a 
planning commissioner to accept a contribution on behalf of a 
councilmember if the contributor is an applicant before the 
planning commission. 
The planning commissioner is the campaign 
manager for the councilmember. 
You asked whether the officer 
might be allowed to accept the contribution because it is on 
benalf of a candidate who is seeking a position that is exempt 
under Section 84308(d). 
The prohibitions of Section 84308 apply to contributions 
accepted, solicited, or directed, for the officer, himself, or 
on behalf of any other candidate for office. 
There is no 
exception for contributions to candidates for positions 
specified in Section 84308(d). 
Similarly, t~e regulation 
defining "solicited" and "directed" does not contain such an 
exception. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.6.) 
Thus, you are 
correct tn concluding that the planning commissioner may not 
solicit or direct a contribution on_behalf of a councilmember. 
Assuming that the planning commissioner does not solicit or 
direct tne contribution, Section 84308 does not prevent an 
applicant oefore tne Planning Commission from making a direct 
contribution to the city councilmember, even though the planning 
commissioner/campaign manager may be aware of it. 
If you have any additional questions, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
~ -~c 
.:.ItU:UJ5 
eLU'I./ 
Jan1s Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY 
(213) 202-5835 
• 
CITY 
OF 
CULVER 
P.O. BOX 507 
CITY 
97:'0 CU~VER BOULEVARD 
CULVER :riY CAliFORNiA 90230 
BERT GLENNON, JR. 
City Attorney 
Barbara Milman 
Chief, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
RE: 
Government Code Section 84308 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
November 29, 1983 
We are writing to solicit your opinion regarding our 
interpretation of the above code section. 
The interpretation set 
forth herein is made in light of the following question: 
Mayan 
appointed officer who serves on a quasi-judicial commission, which 
is not exempted by subdivision (d) of Government Code Section 
84308, solicit, accept or direct a contribution on behalf of an 
officer or candidate for office who serves on a quasi-judicial 
board which is exempted by the aforementioned subdivision. (d)? 
We are prepared at this time to advise that the answer to the 
above question is no. 
However, while this answer seems to be 
consistent with the language of the statute, it also creates an 
anomaly. 
The following example demonstrates what we believe 
constitutes the anomaly: 
A City Planning Commissioner becomes campaign manager 
for a current City Councilmember who is running for 
reelection. 
An applicant comes before the Planning 
Commission and desires to contribute to the Councilman's 
campaign. 
As we read Government Code Section 84308, the Planning 
Commissioner/Campaign Manager may not accept a contribution on 
behalf of the City Councilman from the applicant. 
But the 
applicant can directly contribute to the Councilman's campaign 
without affecting the Planning Commissioner's ability to 
participate in the discussion and vote on the matter. 
It seems 
that under this scenario the influence that Section 84308 is 
attempting to thwart is not prevented. 
If Section 84308 were interpreted to mean that the Planning 
Commissioner could accept the contribution from an applicant on 
behalf of the City Councilman or any candidate other than him or 
herself, this anomaly would net exist. 
This interpretation at 
first seems reasonable because the City Councilman, to whom the 


Barbara Milman 
November 29, 1983 
Page 2 
contribution is made, is not governed by the prohibitions of 
Section 84308. 
However, the language of the last sentence in 
Section 84308(a) does not allow the latter interpretation. 
We 
believe that if the Legislature had intended the last phrase in 
the last sentence of Section 84308(a), ("any other officer, 
alternate, or candidate for office"), to be modified by the 
exemption of subdivision (d), it would have included language 
similar to the qualifying language in the beginning of subdivision 
(a). 
Based on the above, we believe tha~ while it is not entirely 
free from doubt the Planning Commissioner/Campaign Manager is 
prohibited from accepting the contribution. 
We would appreciate your comments regarding our 
interpretations as set forth above. 
We would be pleased to 
further discuss this matter with you in light of the fact that 
while we believe our interpretation to be legally correct, it does 
appear to create a result which somewhat places form over 
substance since there is no prohibition against the applicant 
making a direct campaign contribution which would be known by the 
Commissioner/Campaign Manager. 
As this is a matter of some 
urgency, your prompt consideration would be most appreciated. 
BG:JWP:eh 
LTR/MILMAN/FPPC 
Very truly yours, 
ffi~p'~ 
BERT GLENNON, JR. 
. 
City Attorney

sections.question: May an appointed officer who serves on a quasi-judicial commission, which is not exempted by subdivision (d) of Government Code Section 84308, solicit, accept or direct a contribution on behalf of an officer or candidate for office who serves on a quasi-judicial board which is exempted by the aforementioned subdivision (d)?
sections.conclusion: The planning commissioner may not solicit or direct a contribution on behalf of a councilmember. The prohibitions of Section 84308 apply to contributions accepted, solicited, or directed for any other candidate for office, with no exception for contributions to candidates for positions exempted under Section 84308(d). However, assuming the planning commissioner does not solicit or direct the contribution, Section 84308 does not prevent an applicant before the Planning Commission from making a direct contribution to the city councilmember, even though the planning commissioner/campaign manager may be aware of it.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a planning commissioner (serving on a non-exempt quasi-judicial body) accept, solicit, or direct campaign contributions on behalf of a city councilmember (serving on an exempt body) from an applicant pending before the planning commission?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A planning commissioner cannot solicit or direct contributions on behalf of an exempt councilmember, as Section 84308 prohibits such solicitation or direction regardless of the recipient's exemption status. However, the applicant may make direct contributions to the councilmember without the planning commissioner's involvement.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a complete FPPC advice letter with both the incoming request (November 29, 1983) and FPPC response (January 6, 1984) included in the document. The response clearly addresses the question and provides a definitive conclusion with nuanced guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May an appointed officer who serves on a quasi-judicial commission, which is not exempted by subdivision (d) of Government Code Section 84308, solicit, accept or direct a contribution on behalf of an officer or candidate for office who serves on a quasi-judicial board which is exempted by the aforementioned subdivision (d)?

CONCLUSION: The planning commissioner may not solicit or direct a contribution on behalf of a councilmember. The prohibitions of Section 84308 apply to contributions accepted, solicited, or directed for any other candidate for office, with no exception for contributions to candidates for positions exempted under Section 84308(d). However, assuming the planning commissioner does not solicit or direct the contribution, Section 84308 does not prevent an applicant before the Planning Commission from making a direct contribution to the city councilmember, even though the planning commissioner/campaign manager may be aware of it.

In your letter, you asked whether Section 84308(a) allows a planning commissioner to accept a contribution on behalf of a councilmember if the contributor is an applicant before the planning commission. The planning commissioner is the campaign manager for the councilmember. You asked whether the officer might be allowed to accept the contribution because it is on benalf of a candidate who is seeking a position that is exempt under Section 84308(d). embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a planning commissioner serving as campaign manager for a city councilmember may not solicit or accept contributions on the councilmember's behalf from applicants pending before the planning commission, even though the councilmember's position is exempt from Section 84308 restrictions. The applicant may make direct contributions to the councilmember independently.
